Lakshmanaswami Mudaliar v LIC [1963 | SC | 5 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč United India Life Assurance Co. Ltd., incorporated under the Companies Act, 1882, was registered under the Life 
Insurance Act, 1938 to conduct life insurance business. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In 1955, at an EGM, shareholders resolved to transfer about Rs. 2 lakhs from the Shareholders‚Äô Dividend Account (SDA) to 
a trust. The Company‚Äôs own directors were appointed as trustees. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč With nationalisation, in 1956, LIC took over the company and the act empowered it to recover amounts paid without 
consideration or not necessary for business ‚Üí sought recovery of the 2 L transferred to the trust.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč LIC applied before the Life Insurance Tribunal, which held the directors personally liable. They appealed to the Supreme 
Court. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Although not an IBC case, the transfer resembled an undervalued / preferential transaction: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Directors knew nationalisation was imminent. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč They created a trust with themselves as trustees. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Transfer lacked consideration and was not in the ordinary course of business. 
 
62 
 
Issues: Whether LIC was entitled under Section 15 of the LIC Act, 1956 to recover the Rs. 2 lakh transferred by the Company to the 
trust, on the ground that the transaction was unauthorised, ultra vires, and not reasonably necessary for carrying on life insurance 
business? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Nature of Shareholders‚Äô Dividend Account 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Articles 116‚Äď117 describe SDA as the ‚Äúexclusive property of shareholders,‚ÄĚ but this means only policyholders 
cannot claim it. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Shareholders obtain a right only when dividend is declared. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Until declaration: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
SDA remains the Company‚Äôs asset. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Shareholders have no proprietary or creditor interest in it. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the Company cannot use this money except in accordance with its MoA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Doctrine of Ultra Vires 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A Company: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Can act only within the scope of its objects clause (MoA). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Acts outside MoA are ultra vires, hence: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
completely void, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
cannot be ratified even unanimously by shareholders, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
create no legal rights or liabilities. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 15, LIC Act 
‚ěĘ‚Äč LIC can recover any amount: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Paid without consideration, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Not reasonably necessary for purposes of the insurer‚Äôs business. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Directors‚Äô Liability ‚Äď if directors: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Commit an ultra vires act, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Or divert assets improperly, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč they may be personally liable. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether SDA was shareholders‚Äô property 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Articles 116‚Äď117 only exclude policyholders, they do not confer ownership on shareholders. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Shareholders acquire rights only after declaration of dividend ‚Üí no dividend was declared ‚áí therefore, 
the Rs. 2 lakhs remained the Company‚Äôs asset. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Directors could not treat it as if the shareholders were ‚Äúabsolute owners.‚ÄĚ 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Thus, the resolution donating it to the trust was a disposal of Company property, not shareholders‚Äô 
property. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether MoA authorised creating the trust  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The MoA permitted: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Carrying on insurance business. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
And ‚Äúdoing all such other things as are incidental or conducive to attaining the main objects.‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč However: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The trust created had numerous charitable and educational purposes. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Only one object even remotely related to insurance: promoting technical or business knowledge. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No obligation that the trust funds be applied to insurance education. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even if some money went to insurance education: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
It was far too indirect and remote an advantage. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
It was not reasonably incidental to carrying on life insurance business. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus ‚Üí setting up the trust was wholly outside the Company‚Äôs MoA ‚Üí therefore, the transaction was ultra vires. 
63 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under the ultra vires doctrine: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even unanimous shareholder approval cannot validate it. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Such a transaction is void ab initio. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether directors could avoid liability 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Directors argued: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
AoA permitted donations. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
SDA belonged to shareholders. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Directors acting as trustees should not be personally liable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court rejected all arguments: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
AoA cannot override MoA. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Donation was ultra vires the Company, and hence void. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Directors knew or should have known that the trust had no connection to the Company‚Äôs objects. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
They acted in breach of duty by transferring Company assets without authority. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, personal liability was justified. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Why LIC could claw back under Section 15 ‚Üí the donation was: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Without consideration, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Not reasonably necessary for carrying on life insurance business, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Ultra vires, hence void from inception. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Therefore, LIC was entitled to a refund of the Rs. 2 lakhs. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Company‚Äôs act of creating and funding the trust was ultra vires the MoA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SDA funds belonged to the Company, not shareholders, until dividends were declared. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The transfer was therefore an unauthorised and invalid disposal of Company property. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč LIC was entitled under Section 15 of the LIC Act to recover the Rs. 2 lakhs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Directors were held personally liable, since they acted beyond their authority. 
 
Piramal v 63 Moons [2025 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč DHFL is major housing finance NBFC lending to retail borrowers and builders; raised funds through loans, ECBs, NCDs, 
subordinated/perpetual debt, deposits, and borrowings from banks, FIs, insurers, MFs, PFs, pension funds, and individuals. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Accused of large-scale siphoning, fake borrowers, shell entities, and diversion of funds. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RBI superseded the board; CIRP admitted. NCLT received ~‚āĻ82,247 crore in claims; identified ~‚āĻ45,050 crore of 
potentially avoidable / Section 66 transactions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Revised RFRP required specific treatment of Section 66 recoveries.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Piramal‚Äôs final RP (~‚āĻ37,250 crore) stated such recoveries would go to the SRA.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
CoC (77 creditors) approved with 93.65 percent voting. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT filed for approval ‚Üí 63 Moons objected to the Section 66 clause, but NCLT approved the plan and dismissed the 
objection.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč A separate direction to consider an ex-director‚Äôs second settlement proposal was stayed on appeal. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT struck down the clause giving Section 66 recoveries to the SRA and remanded the plan to the CoC; other appeals 
addressed distribution and locus. 
 
Issues: Whether the resolution plan approved by the CoC and NCLT was in contravention of any law, thereby justifying the exercise 
of NCLAT‚Äôs jurisdiction under Section 61 of the IBC? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 25(2)(j), Chapter III ‚Äď Avoidance Transactions ‚Üí RP must mandatorily file applications for avoidance transactions: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Preferential ‚Äď S.43 
64 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Undervalued ‚Äď S.45 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Extortionate credit ‚Äď S.50 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 26: Filing of avoidance applications does not stop CIRP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 66 ‚Äď Fraudulent & Wrongful Trading (Chapter VI) ‚Äď  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Applies where business of CD was carried on: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
With intent to defraud creditors 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Or for fraudulent purposes 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The AA does not ‚Äúavoid‚ÄĚ the transaction; it orders persons responsible to contribute to the assets of the CD. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 30(2) and Regulation 38 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Mandatory content of RP ‚Üí must comply with statutory requirements; failing which NCLT must reject. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Sections 31 & 61 ‚Äď Approval & Appellate Review 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT: limited to checking compliance with S.30(2). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT under S.61(3): grounds restricted to: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Material irregularity 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Non-compliance with substantive provisions 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Improper constitution of CoC 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Contravention of law 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Cannot interfere with CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 37 & Maximization Principle ‚Äď RP must include measures for maximizing the value of CD‚Äôs assets. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RBI Act & NHB Act ‚Äď No provision mandating full repayment of deposits; no statutory override over IBC distribution 
waterfall. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Distinction between Avoidance Applications (Ch. III) and Fraud/Wrongful Trading (Ch. VI) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC stressed a clear statutory separation: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Avoidance applications (S. 43, 45, 50) ‚Üí avoid transactions ‚Üí property identifiable ‚Üí set aside or 
restore. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Fraudulent/wrongful trading (S.66) ‚Üí no avoidance ‚Üí persons wrongfully carrying on business 
contribute to assets ‚Üí property may be unidentifiable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Legislature deliberately excluded S.66 from S.25 RP obligations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT erred by treating S.66 applications like avoidance applications. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RP Clause Allowing SRA to Appropriate Recoveries 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CoC in its commercial wisdom approved such appropriation + NCLT approved the RP ‚áí NCLAT had no 
jurisdiction to tinker with an isolated clause, especially when: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The plan was overwhelmingly approved 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Creditors challenging it had themselves voted in favour 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Conduct of 63 Moons & Roopjyot Engineering 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Both were part of classes of creditors whose Authorized Representative voted in favour ‚Üí binds the entire class ‚Üí 
estopped from objecting ‚áí to allow challenges would destroy finality and stall large CIRPs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 37A (Liquidation Regulations) Misapplied 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT wrongly relied on Regulation 37A (Liquidator‚Äôs power to assign non-realizable assets) ‚Üí CIRP is 
governed by CIRP Regulations, not Liquidation Regulations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence reliance was ‚Äúthoroughly misplaced.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Maximization of Value & CoC‚Äôs Commercial Wisdom 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Maximization is undefined but built into the structure of RP evaluation + measures under Regulation 37 were 
placed before CoC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CoC found the plan feasible, viable, and commercially preferable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Judicial review is thus minimal; NCLAT exceeded its limits. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Alleged Violations of RBI Act / NHB Act 
65 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Deposit protection provisions under these Acts do not mandate full payment or create priority outside IBC 
waterfall. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No authority under these Acts issued any order requiring full repayment. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence no ‚Äúcontravention of law‚ÄĚ under S.61(3). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Avoidance Applications Still Pending 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC noted that approx. ‚āĻ45,050 crore avoidance applications were pending. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Their outcome does not stall CIRP and is separate from the question of who receives recoveries. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Vesting of avoidance recoveries is a commercial decision of CoC. 
 
Conclusion: The Supreme Court held that: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT had no jurisdiction under Section 61 to interfere with or modify an isolated clause of an RP approved by CoC and 
NCLT. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Avoidance applications under S.43, 45, 50 and fraudulent/wrongful trading under S.66 are conceptually and statutorily 
distinct. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The RP did not violate the RBI Act or NHB Act. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Creditors who voted in favour (including those represented through ARs) are bound and estopped from challenging the 
RP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 37A (Liquidation Regulations) does not apply to CIRP.
